title: Putting a face to the homeless
summary: Tribe offers support services, but resources are limited amid strong demand Surrounded by her belongings, Eunice Ruhl talks about her experience living as a squatter in an abandoned cabin near Kenai. The cabin has no electricity or running water. She has improvised a woodstove for heat and built an outhouse nearby. Barehanded, breath visible, Eunice Ruhl knelt down, brushed snow off the log and propped it upright. With a grunt, she slung an ax over her shoulder and thrust it toward the ground. The blade thumped into the wood, which crumbled into chunks the size of charcoal briquettes. “Pretty rotten,” Ruhl said, catching her breath, “but it will do.” To stay warm that night, it would have to do. Ruhl is homeless and living on the central Kenai Peninsula, the tribe’s service area, a region known for its natural beauty and world-class outdoor recreation....
